Index Disclosures

MSCI ACWI (net) was stated as the primary benchmark on June 1, 2024 and MSCI World (net) became the secondary benchmark. The MSCI ACWI captures large and mid-cap representation across 23 developed markets and 24 emerging markets countries. With approximately 2,700 constituents, the Index covers approximately 85% of the global investable equity opportunity set. The MSCI ACWI Equal Weighted Index represents an alternative weighting scheme to its market cap weighted parent index, MSCI ACWI. The Index includes the same constituents as its parent (large and mid-cap securities from 23 developed markets and 24 emerging markets countries. However, at each quarterly rebalance date, all index constituents are weighted equally, effectively removing the influence of each constituent's current price (high or low). Between rebalances, index constituent weightings will fluctuate due to price performance. The MSCI World Index is a free float-adjusted market capitalization weighted index that is designed to measure the equity market performance of developed markets. The MSCI World Index consists of the following 23 developed market country indexes: Australia, Austria, Belgium, Canada, Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, Hong Kong, Ireland, Israel, Italy, Japan, Netherlands, New Zealand, Norway, Portugal, Singapore, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, the United Kingdom and the United States. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index is a free float-adjusted market capitalization-weighted index that is designed to measure equity market performance of emerging markets. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index consists of the following 24 emerging market country indexes: Brazil, Chile, China, Colombia, Czech Republic, Egypt, Greece, Hungary, India, Indonesia, Korea, Kuwait, Malaysia, Mexico, Peru, Philippines, Poland, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, South Africa, Taiwan, Thailand, Turkey and United Arab Emirates. The MSCI ACWI Growth Index captures large and mid-cap securities exhibiting overall growth style characteristics across 23 developed markets countries and 24 emerging markets countries. The MSCI ACWI Value Index captures large and mid-cap securities exhibiting overall value style characteristics across 23 developed markets countries and 24 emerging markets countries. The MSCI Europe Index captures large and mid-cap representation across 15 developed markets countries in Europe. With more than 400 constituents, the Index covers approximately 85% of the free float-adjusted market capitalization across the European developed markets equity universe. The MSCI Japan Index is designed to measure the performance of the large and mid-cap segments of the Japanese market. With approximately 200 constituents, the Index covers approximately 85% of the free float-adjusted market capitalization in Japan. The S&P 500® Index is the Standard & Poor's Composite Index of 500 stocks and is a widely recognized, unmanaged index of common stock prices. The Bloomberg Global Aggregate Bond Index is a flagship measure of global investment grade debt from 28 local currency markets. This multi-currency benchmark includes Treasury, government-related, corporate and securitized fixed rate bonds from both developed and emerging markets issuers. The Brent Crude Oil Index is a major trading classification of sweet light crude oil that serves as a major benchmark price for purchases of oil worldwide. The volatility (beta) of the Composite may be greater or less than the benchmarks. It is not possible to invest directly in these indexes.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Pine Cliff Energy: 6% Dividend Yield While Waiting For Higher Prices
data.description:
Introduction

Pine Cliff Energy (TSX:PNE:CA) (OTCQX:PIFYF) is a Canadian small cap focusing on the production of natural gas. As the natgas price has been pretty low throughout the summer, Pine Cliff’s share price has suffered but one could also see this as a good setup going into the winter period where natural gas prices tend to be higher. As it has been a while since I discussed Pine Cliff Energy, I felt this might be an opportune time to have a closer look at Pine Cliff as a speculative call option on this winter’s natural gas price.

The low natural gas price is certainly hurting the company

Pine Cliff’s total consolidated production is approximately 23,700 barrels of oil-equivalent per day, of which 3,334 barrels were Natural Gas Liquids and 1,599 barrels per day consisted of oil. This indeed means natural gas represents close to 80% of the total oil-equivalent production. However, due to the low natural gas price, it only generates 44% of the total revenue, as you can see below. That’s a blessing in disguise as it means the higher oil and NGL prices are protecting the company’s downside.

The average realized price was C$2.10 per Mcf of natural gas during the second quarter, but investors should have no illusions: the natural gas price continued to decrease throughout the summer months and one should expect a very weak third quarter result although the relatively strong oil price during the quarter will have been helpful.

The total revenue during the second quarter came in at C$48.3M and approximately C$46.6M after taking the impact of the royalty payments as well as the processing and gathering revenue into account. The total amount of operating expenses was approximately C$51.9M, of which just over a quarter was due to the depletion and depreciation expenses. That being said, as the income statement below shows, the total amount spent on operating and transportation expenses represented about 2/3rd of the total revenue. And whereas other natural gas producers are in a strong position with the NGL and oil revenue covering all operating expenses, Pine Cliff doesn’t have that luxury.

So even at a natural gas price of C$2.10, Pine Cliff reported a net loss of approximately C$4.1M which represents roughly C$0.01 per share based on the share count of approximately 355M shares.

Fortunately the company’s cash flow statement paints a slightly better picture with an operating cash flow of C$5.7M on a reported basis, including a C$4.3M working capital investment and excluding C$0.3M in lease payments. On an adjusted basis, the operating cash flow was approximately C$9.7M during the second quarter.

One of the best features of Pine Cliff is its ability to just shut down its investment program thanks to the relatively low decline rate of its older assets. The total capex was just over C$1M which means that despite the low prices and the reported net loss, the company was free cash flow positive to the tune of C$8.7M or C$0.0245/share. This also means the current dividend of C$0.005 per month remains covered.

For now, Pine Cliff has kept its full-year capex guidance unchanged at C$17.5M but given the consistently low natural gas prices, I think the C$7M in development spending may be deferred until 2025 as Pine Cliff’s cash flows remain under pressure. Excluding the potentially deferred capex, the 2024 capex program would be around C$10.5M of which C$1.6M has been spent in the first half of the year.

With an average spot price of the AECO natural gas of just around C$1/Mcf throughout the summer, these are difficult times for natural gas producers. Fortunately Pine Cliff has hedged about 45% of its natural gas production at C$2.87/Mcf so even if the realized price on the open market would be just C$1, the average realized price would still be around C$1.80. This would have a negative effect of around C$3M on the revenue and the operating cash flow on a quarterly basis.

So while I expect Q3 to be bad, Pine Cliff’s management has taken solid steps to reduce the impact on its cash flow performance. Pine Cliff had also already hedged about a third of its Q1 production at around C$3, and I can certainly appreciate a good hedging strategy. Additionally, not all of the natural gas will be subject to AECO prices as approximately 35% of the anticipated 2025 production is not priced at AECO.

Investment thesis

I still consider Pine Cliff to be an excellent call option on the natural gas price as even a natgas price of C$3/Mcf (which is not outrageous at all) would boost the operating cash flow to around C$65M and even if you’d include a C$20M capex program, a C$3 natgas price would generate C$45M in free cash flow while even the current dividend of C$0.005/month would be handsomely covered.

I currently have no position in Pine Cliff but I may initiate a long position in the coming weeks, as a speculative bet on the AECO natural gas price gaining strength over the winter. And hopefully Pine Cliff will continue to be able to expand its hedge book at higher natgas prices.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
